narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.30—below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 26, which is notably lower than the dataset average of 36.70. The admissions model predicted a 92% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, a threshold that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction wasn't random; it was driven primarily by three key factors from your academic record, ranked by their influence on pushing the model toward this unfavorable outcome. The strongest factor was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year—far below the average decile of about 5.5—which strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar exam struggles, as lower rankings like yours consistently correlate with first-time failures in the data. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which, being well below average, further pulled the prediction toward failure by indicating potential challenges with the rigorous reasoning demands of law school and the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also in the bottom 10% compared to the typical 5.7 average, rounded out the top three, reinforcing a pattern of early underperformance that the model saw as a continued liability. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles and score around 37 on the LSAT, your metrics across these areas placed you in a riskier group, tipping the overall balance despite positives like your full-time commitment, which is the norm for 93% of students. While factors like family income didn't sway things much here, these academic indicators dominated the decision. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your grades in any ongoing studies to climb those decile rankings, and perhaps seeking targeted prep courses or tutoring proven to help students from similar backgrounds succeed on the bar. Many applicants in your position have rebounded by addressing these exact gaps, so persistence could open doors at other programs or in future cycles.
